description: Deep reference for problem decomposition strategies — the most under-appreciated source of genuine solution diversity.
usage: Use when two paths share the same paradigm and data structure. The decomposition strategy chosen determines how the problem is framed, making the algorithms fundamentally different.

strategies.name: Offline vs. Online
strategies.coreReframe: Offline — read all queries before answering any of them, then reorder, batch, or preprocess to answer optimally. Online — answer each query as it arrives, with no future knowledge.
structuralSignals: The problem gives you all queries upfront (offline is available)
Queries arrive in a stream or order matters for correctness (online required)
The offline approach would require a data structure that's hard to implement online (e.g., persistent or retroactive)
whatItUnlocks: Offline enables sorting queries by a key (endpoint, timestamp, value) to sweep through them in a single pass, answering each as you go
Offline enables 'reverse time' tricks: process deletions as additions in reverse order
Offline enables divide-and-conquer on queries (parallel binary search, CDQ divide & conquer)
Online forces incremental data structures (segment trees, BITs, DSU) but avoids the overhead of sorting and reordering

classicExamples.examples: Count inversions (sort + merge), range query on static array (Mo's algorithm), offline LCA (Tarjan's), offline range median
LRU cache, stock price tracker, median of data stream
For each query (L, R), count elements in range with value in [A, B] -> offline sort by R, sweep with BIT; online version requires persistent segment tree

failureModes: Offline reorder destroys the causal structure the problem requires (e.g., 'process queries where each query modifies state for the next')
Sorting queries changes which data is 'visible' at query time in a way that's hard to reconstruct

strategies.name: Forward vs. Reverse Processing
strategies.coreReframe: Process the input from right-to-left (or end-to-start) instead of the natural left-to-right order. Equivalently: define DP state based on 'suffix' rather than 'prefix.'
structuralSignals: A forward pass requires knowing the future (e.g., 'does any element to the right exceed X?')
The recurrence is cleaner when the base case is the end rather than the start
A 'next occurrence' or 'future event' query appears in the problem
whatItUnlocks: Forward processing accumulates past information; reverse processing accumulates future information
Some DP transitions that require O(n) lookahead forward become O(1) backward
'Next greater element' is trivially computed right-to-left with a stack; left-to-right requires more bookkeeping

classicExamples.examples: Jump game: Forward greedy reaches O(n); backward DP can_reach[i] = any(can_reach[j] for j reachable from i) is also O(n) but reverse
Candy distribution: Two-pass — left-to-right for 'greater than left neighbor', then right-to-left for 'greater than right neighbor'; neither pass alone suffices
Delete edges offline: Process deletions as additions in reverse; DSU handles additions but not deletions
Suffix array / suffix automaton: Fundamentally reverse-oriented structures

failureModes: Reverse processing changes the order of side effects (mutation problems)
Some monotone properties hold forward but not backward (check the invariant holds in reverse)

strategies.name: Problem Transformation / Reduction
strategies.coreReframe: Map the problem to a different, well-known problem where efficient algorithms already exist. Solve the transformed problem, then map the answer back.
structuralSignals: The problem description is unusual but reminds you structurally of something familiar
A change of variables simplifies the recurrence or constraint
The problem can be encoded as a graph, sorted array, or algebraic structure
whatItUnlocks: Transforms often reduce time complexity by applying known optimal algorithms
A hard constraint in one framing may be trivial in another
Transformation can convert a 2D problem to 1D, or an online problem to an offline one

commonTransformationPatterns.transformTo: Edit distance (different DP on same structure)

commonTransformationPatterns.why: Reduces dimension when moves are constrained

commonTransformationPatterns.transformTo: Sort by endpoint + greedy or sweep
Prefix sum difference: prefix[r] - prefix[l] = K
commonTransformationPatterns.why: Converts range query to point lookup

commonTransformationPatterns.transformTo: Prefix XOR: xor(l, r) = prefix[r] ^ prefix[l-1]
commonTransformationPatterns.why: Same as prefix sum but for XOR

commonTransformationPatterns.transformTo: Complement counting: total - pairs NOT satisfying

commonTransformationPatterns.originalFraming: Minimum of maximum or maximum of minimum

classicExamples.examples: Minimum time to complete all tasks -> Is it possible in T time? -> binary search on T
Count subarrays with sum K is hard directly; after prefix sum it becomes count pairs (i,j) where prefix[j] - prefix[i] = K — solvable with a hashmap in O(n)
LIS length equals minimum number of piles in patience sort; transforms sequence problem into geometric one

failureModes: The transformation is more complex than a direct solution for the given constraints
The inverse mapping from transformed answer to original answer is lossy or ambiguous

strategies.name: Primal vs. Dual / Complement Framing
strategies.coreReframe: Instead of counting or optimizing what you want, count the complement and subtract, or solve the dual formulation and invert.
structuralSignals: Counting directly is hard because valid configurations are irregular
The invalid or excluded configurations have simpler structure
Min-max duality applies (maximize one thing <-> minimize its complement)
whatItUnlocks: Inclusion-exclusion: count all, subtract overcounts
'At least one' is hard; 'none' is often a product of independent probabilities
'Maximum independent set' is hard; 'minimum vertex cover' = n - maximum matching (Konig's theorem on bipartite graphs)
'Minimum cut' = 'maximum flow' (max-flow min-cut theorem)

classicExamples.examples: Count subarrays with at most K distinct -> use 'at most K' - 'at most K-1' = 'exactly K'
Probability of at least one collision -> 1 - P(no collision) (birthday paradox)
Minimum cost to make array non-decreasing -> dual is maximum number of elements you can keep (LIS)
Find duplicate by XOR -> XOR all elements + all indices cancels everything except the duplicate
Minimum path cover in DAG -> n - maximum bipartite matching

failureModes: Complement set is not simpler than the original (both are irregular)
Inclusion-exclusion has too many terms (use DP or Mobius inversion instead)

strategies.name: Eager vs. Lazy Evaluation
strategies.coreReframe: Eager — compute and propagate results immediately when state changes. Lazy — defer work until it's actually needed; batch deferred updates before queries.
structuralSignals: Many updates affect a range of elements (lazy is efficient: one deferred tag covers the range)
Updates and queries are interleaved and the full result isn't needed until later
Some updates will be overwritten before they're ever queried (lazy avoids wasted work)
whatItUnlocks: Lazy propagation on segment trees: range update in O(log n) instead of O(n) by pushing the update tag down only when a child is accessed
Lazy deletion in heaps/sorted structures: mark elements as invalid at O(1), pay the cleanup cost only at pop time
Memoization is lazy DP: compute only the subproblems actually needed vs. bottom-up tabulation (eager, computes all)
Event-driven simulation is lazy: advance state only when an event occurs vs. simulating every tick

classicExamples.examples: Range add + range sum query; without lazy, range add is O(n); with lazy tag it's O(log n)
Lazy heap in Dijkstra: Instead of decreasing key (complex), push a new entry and skip stale pops
Lazy deletion in interval scheduling: Mark processed intervals as used; skip them on next access
Memoized DFS vs. BFS: Top-down memoization (lazy) explores only reachable states; BFS (eager) explores all states at each level

failureModes: Lazy tags must be composable: if you apply 'add 5' then 'multiply by 2', the composed lazy tag is 'multiply by 2, add 10' — non-composable updates require careful ordering
Lazy deletion requires a validity check at every pop; forgetting this produces wrong answers silently

strategies.name: Push vs. Pull Propagation
strategies.coreReframe: Push — when a node's value changes, it immediately notifies and updates all its dependents. Pull — when a node needs its value, it requests it from its dependencies on demand.
structuralSignals: Clear producer/consumer structure in the problem (push: producer drives; pull: consumer drives)
In-degree vs. out-degree asymmetry: high out-degree -> pushing is expensive; high in-degree -> pulling is expensive
DP on DAGs: 'which direction do transitions flow?'
whatItUnlocks: Push-based DP: dp[v] = f(dp[u]) — for each node u, update all successors v. Natural for topological-order DP where each node 'contributes to' its children
Pull-based DP: dp[v] = g(dp[u] for u in predecessors(v)) — each node 'collects from' its parents. Natural when a node needs all parent values before it can be computed
Push works well for sparse graphs (low out-degree); pull works well when parents are few
Tree DP rerooting: compute subtree answers (pull), then reroot to compute full-tree answers (push back down)

classicExamples.examples: Push in BFS: When popping node u, push u's contribution to all neighbors v
Pull in Dijkstra: When processing node v, pull the best distance from all incoming edges
Push DP in coin change: For each coin value c, for each amount i, update dp[i+c]
Pull DP in coin change: For each amount i, check all coins c and pull dp[i-c]
Rerooting DP: First pass pulls subtree answers bottom-up; second pass pushes 'upward' contribution top-down

failureModes: Push requires knowing all outgoing edges at update time (bad for implicit graphs)
Pull requires knowing all incoming edges at query time (bad when in-degree is large and irregular)

strategies.name: Divide the Domain (Meet in the Middle / Splitting)
strategies.coreReframe: Instead of searching the full space of size S, split the problem into two halves of size sqrt(S), solve each independently, then combine. Typically reduces exponential to sub-exponential or O(n^2) to O(n*sqrt(n)) or O(n log n).
structuralSignals: Brute force is O(2^n) or O(n^2) and n is ~40 (meet in middle) or the constraint is large but the structure is symmetric
The problem has a 'balance point' — combining a left-half result with a right-half result is efficient
Two independent partial solutions can be joined by a single key (sum, XOR, sorted position)
whatItUnlocks: 4-sum, subset sum with N=40: split into two halves of N=20 (2^20 each); combine via hash or binary search
Bidirectional BFS: expand from both source and target; total states explored ~ 2 * b^(d/2) instead of b^d
CDQ divide and conquer: offline 2D/3D problem solved by splitting on one dimension, solving each half, counting cross contributions at the split

classicExamples.examples: Subset sum N=40: Brute force O(2^40); meet in middle O(2^20 * 20) — feasible
Bidirectional BFS on word ladder: Bidirectional reduces from 26^n to 2 * 26^(n/2)
4-sum: Split into all (a+b) pairs and all (c+d) pairs; look up complement in hash map

failureModes: The combination step is expensive (O(n) per pair -> no asymptotic gain)
The two halves aren't truly independent (shared state must be carefully threaded through)

strategies.name: Coordinate / Dimension Reduction
strategies.coreReframe: Project a multi-dimensional problem onto fewer dimensions, or compress a large sparse coordinate space into a dense one, to enable efficient algorithms that only work in 1D or on small ranges.
structuralSignals: The problem involves 2D or 3D geometry but movement is constrained to one axis at a time
Values are large but the number of distinct values is small
A 2D DP can be reduced to 1D by fixing one dimension and sweeping
whatItUnlocks: Coordinate compression: map N distinct values in [1, 10^9] to [1, N] to use a Fenwick tree
Sweep line: fix one axis, sweep along the other, answer queries at each position
1D projection of 2D DP: when the DP table's row depends only on the previous row, use a rolling array
Row-by-row or column-by-column processing of grids: treat each row as a bitmask to apply bitmask DP in 2D grid

classicExamples.examples: Count inversions: Map values to [1, N]; use Fenwick tree on compressed coordinates
Rectangle intersection: Sweep left-to-right; maintain active set of y-intervals
Maximal rectangle in histogram: Reduce 2D matrix to 1D histogram row by row
2D bitmask DP: Broken profile DP for tiling — state is the bitmask of the current column's protrusions

failureModes: Compression destroys ordering structure needed by the algorithm (check that comparisons remain valid after compression)
The sweep line event structure is complex to implement correctly (missed or double-counted events)

strategies.name: Amortization and Potential
strategies.coreReframe: Design an operation that is occasionally expensive but amortized cheap — pay for expensive steps by 'banking' credit during cheap steps. The potential method formalizes this: assign a potential phi to each state; amortized cost = actual cost + delta-phi.
structuralSignals: A sequence of operations has variable cost but the total is bounded
The expensive operations become possible only after many cheap operations (e.g., rebuilding a structure)
You want to claim O(1) per operation even though some individual operations are O(n)
whatItUnlocks: Dynamic arrays (append): O(1) amortized because doubling is rare
Union-Find with path compression: O(alpha(n)) amortized
Splay trees / move-to-front: amortized O(log n) per access
Stack-based algorithms with 'push each element once, pop each element once' invariant: total O(n) even though individual pops look like O(n)

classicExamples.examples: Monotonic stack (amortized argument): Each element is pushed once and popped once -> O(n) total despite appearances
Two-pointer (amortized argument): Left pointer and right pointer each move at most N times -> O(n) total
Tarjan's SCC: Each node is pushed to and popped from the stack exactly once -> O(V + E)

failureModes: Amortized analysis doesn't apply when individual worst-case latency matters (real-time systems)
The amortized bound requires the sequence to start from an 'empty' state; if the structure is pre-loaded, the analysis changes

strategies.name: Boundary Isolation
strategies.coreReframe: Isolate concerns at process, module, or network boundaries so that each side can evolve, deploy, and test independently. The boundary defines the contract; everything behind it is an implementation detail.
structuralSignals: Cross-process communication patterns emerging (IPC, HTTP, message passing)
Module coupling making changes ripple across the codebase
Test isolation needs: tests for one module break when another module changes
whatItUnlocks: Independent deployment: change one side of the boundary without redeploying the other
Parallel development: teams work on different sides of the boundary simultaneously
Test sandboxing: mock one side of the boundary for fast, deterministic tests
Technology heterogeneity: different languages or frameworks on each side

classicExamples.examples: Microservice boundaries: each service owns its data and exposes an API; internal implementation is opaque
Electron main/renderer split: main process handles OS access, renderer handles UI; IPC bridge connects them
Module federation: independently built and deployed frontend modules loaded at runtime

failureModes: Over-isolation: too many boundaries create coordination overhead, distributed system complexity, and latency
Leaky abstractions: implementation details seep through the boundary (shared database, coupled schemas)
Boundary mismatch: the boundary doesn't align with the natural seams in the domain, causing cross-boundary transactions

strategies.name: Fixture and Seed
strategies.coreReframe: Generate test data through factories, builders, or snapshot seeding rather than constructing it inline in every test. Centralize data creation to keep tests readable, maintainable, and deterministic.
structuralSignals: Test data setup is verbose and duplicated across test files
Tests need reproducible state that is complex to construct manually
Complex object graphs with relationships that must be consistent
whatItUnlocks: Deterministic tests: same factory call produces same data every time
Fast setup: pre-built snapshots or seed data avoid expensive construction per test
Readable test code: factory calls express intent ('createActiveUser') rather than implementation details
Maintainable data: schema changes are absorbed in one place (the factory) rather than every test

classicExamples.examples: factory-bot (Ruby), Fishery (TS): define a factory per model with sensible defaults and override capability
Test data builders: fluent API — new UserBuilder().withEmail('x').withRole('admin').build()
Database snapshots: dump a known-good state, restore before each test suite for integration tests
Fixture files (JSON/YAML): static data files loaded per test; good for API response mocking

failureModes: Brittle fixtures coupled to schema: every schema change breaks fixtures; use factories with defaults instead
Snapshot rot: snapshots become stale as the schema evolves; automate snapshot regeneration
Factory proliferation: too many specialized factories; use traits or overrides on a base factory

strategies.name: Environment Override
strategies.coreReframe: Inject configuration at runtime by overriding environment variables, file paths, or config layers rather than changing code. The same binary behaves differently based on its environment.
structuralSignals: Multi-environment deployment: dev, staging, production need different settings
Test vs production config: tests need isolated databases, mock endpoints, or feature flags
Feature flags controlling behavior without code changes or redeployment
whatItUnlocks: Environment portability: same artifact runs anywhere with the right config
Test isolation: override DATABASE_URL, API_BASE, or feature flags per test suite
Gradual rollout: enable features for a percentage of users via config, not code branches
Debugging in production: toggle verbose logging or debug endpoints via config

classicExamples.examples: dotenv cascades: .env.local > .env.test > .env with per-environment overrides
Config layering: base config merged with environment-specific overrides using deep merge
Path redirection for tests: override DATA_DIR to a temp directory for filesystem isolation

failureModes: Env var sprawl: too many env vars with unclear naming; use a config schema to document and validate
Missing overrides in production: forgetting to set a required env var; fail fast with validation at startup
Config drift between environments: staging and production diverge silently; use config-as-code with diff tooling

strategies.name: Denormalization for Read
strategies.coreReframe: Trade write complexity for read performance by precomputing and storing derived data. Accept data duplication to eliminate expensive joins, aggregations, or computations at query time.
structuralSignals: Slow queries dominated by joins across multiple tables
Join-heavy reads that could be precomputed once and read many times
Reporting or analytics workloads with complex aggregations
whatItUnlocks: Fast reads: single-table lookups replace multi-table joins
Reduced query complexity: precomputed columns eliminate runtime calculations
Materialized views: database-managed denormalization with automatic or manual refresh
Read replicas: separate read-optimized copies with denormalized schemas

classicExamples.examples: Denormalized tables: store user_name on order rows instead of joining to users table on every read
Materialized views: CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_revenue AS SELECT ... WITH DATA; REFRESH periodically
Computed columns: store total_price = quantity * unit_price instead of calculating per query
Read replicas: route reporting queries to a replica with denormalized indexes and views

failureModes: Stale data: denormalized values become inconsistent with source; define refresh strategy (trigger, schedule, event-driven)
Write amplification: every write to the source must update all denormalized copies; measure write overhead
Consistency gaps: application code updates the source but forgets to update the denormalized copy

strategies.name: Schema Evolution
strategies.coreReframe: Evolve database or API schemas incrementally while maintaining backward compatibility with existing consumers. Every change must be deployable without breaking running systems.
structuralSignals: Backward compatibility requirements: old clients must continue working during and after migration
Migration risk: schema changes could break production if not carefully sequenced
Multiple consumers: different services or client versions depend on the same schema
whatItUnlocks: Zero-downtime schema changes: deploy schema and code changes independently
Gradual migration: old and new schema coexist during transition period
Version coexistence: multiple schema versions serve different consumers simultaneously
Confident rollback: schema changes are reversible without data loss

classicExamples.examples: Expand-contract pattern: add new column (expand), migrate data, update code, drop old column (contract) across releases
API versioning: /v1/users and /v2/users served simultaneously; v1 deprecated on timeline
Backward-compatible migrations: add columns with defaults, never rename in a single step, use views as abstraction layer

failureModes: Breaking changes in a 'compatible' release: removing a column that old code still reads
Orphaned columns: expand phase completes but contract phase is never executed; dead columns accumulate
Migration rollback failures: down migration doesn't restore data that was transformed during up migration

strategies.name: API Versioning Strategy
strategies.coreReframe: Manage multiple API versions to give consumers a stable contract while allowing the API to evolve. The versioning strategy determines how breaking changes are communicated, how long old versions live, and how clients migrate.
structuralSignals: Breaking API change required but existing clients cannot update immediately
Multiple client versions in the wild (mobile apps, third-party integrations)
Need for a deprecation policy with defined sunset dates
whatItUnlocks: Backward compatibility: old clients continue working during migration window
Controlled deprecation: sunset dates and migration guides for each version
Client migration timeline: consumers move at their own pace within the support window
Independent API and client release cycles

classicExamples.examples: URL path versioning: /api/v1/users, /api/v2/users — simple, visible, cacheable
Header versioning: Accept: application/vnd.myapp.v2+json — keeps URLs clean
Content negotiation: client requests a specific representation; server serves the best match

failureModes: Version proliferation: too many active versions; define maximum supported versions and sunset policy
Indefinite support burden: old versions never sunset; set and enforce deprecation timelines
Inconsistent behavior across versions: v1 and v2 diverge in subtle ways; shared test suites prevent drift

strategies.name: Consistency Trade-off
strategies.coreReframe: Choose the consistency level that matches business requirements rather than defaulting to strong consistency everywhere. Weaker consistency enables better performance, availability, and partition tolerance.
structuralSignals: Distributed state: data replicated across nodes or regions
Latency vs correctness tension: strong consistency adds round-trip latency
Business rules that tolerate eventual consistency (social feeds, view counts, recommendations)
whatItUnlocks: Performance in distributed systems: reads from local replica without cross-region round-trip
Partition tolerance: system continues operating during network splits
Read scaling: any replica can serve reads under eventual consistency
Tunable consistency: per-query consistency level based on the operation's requirements

classicExamples.examples: Strong consistency (2PC): bank transfers, inventory reservation — correctness is non-negotiable
Eventual consistency (CRDT): collaborative editing, shopping cart merge, distributed counters
Read-your-writes: user sees their own changes immediately; other users see changes eventually
Causal consistency: if A causes B, everyone sees A before B; concurrent events may appear in any order

failureModes: Phantom reads: reading stale data that leads to incorrect business decisions
Lost updates: concurrent writes overwrite each other without conflict detection
Split-brain decisions: partitioned nodes make conflicting decisions; use fencing tokens or consensus protocols

strategies.name: Idempotency Design
strategies.coreReframe: Ensure operations produce the same result regardless of how many times they are executed. Safe retries, exactly-once semantics, and fault tolerance all depend on idempotent operations.
structuralSignals: Retry logic in the system: network failures trigger automatic retries
At-least-once delivery: message queues may deliver the same message multiple times
Duplicate requests: users double-click, clients retry on timeout, load balancers replay
whatItUnlocks: Safe retries: any failed operation can be retried without side effects
Exactly-once semantics: at-least-once delivery + idempotent consumer = exactly-once processing
Fault tolerance: crashes during processing are recovered by re-executing the same operation
Simplified error handling: no need to distinguish 'already done' from 'succeeded'

classicExamples.examples: Idempotency keys: client sends a unique key per request; server deduplicates by key (Stripe API pattern)
Deduplication tables: store processed message IDs; skip if already seen
Tombstone records: soft-delete with a marker rather than physical delete; re-delete is a no-op
Conditional writes: UPDATE ... WHERE version = N; only succeeds if state hasn't changed

failureModes: Non-idempotent side effects: sending emails, charging credit cards, or incrementing counters on retry
Key collision: idempotency keys reused across different logical operations
Stale deduplication cache: evicting processed IDs too early allows duplicate processing

strategies.name: Hydration Strategy
strategies.coreReframe: Choose when and how client-side JavaScript activates server-rendered HTML. The hydration strategy controls the trade-off between initial paint speed, time-to-interactive, and JavaScript bundle size.
structuralSignals: SSR performance concerns: server renders HTML but client-side JS takes too long to activate it
Time-to-interactive is significantly later than first contentful paint
JavaScript bundle size is large relative to the interactivity it provides
whatItUnlocks: Faster initial paint: server-rendered HTML is visible before any JS executes
Reduced JS overhead: partial or selective hydration skips non-interactive regions
Selective interactivity: only hydrate components that need client-side behavior
Streaming: progressive delivery of HTML and selective hydration of arrived components

classicExamples.examples: Full hydration: server renders HTML, client re-renders the entire tree to attach event handlers (React SSR default)
Partial hydration: only interactive 'islands' are hydrated; static content remains server-rendered HTML (Astro)
Progressive hydration: defer hydration of below-fold components until they enter viewport or become idle
Resumability (Qwik): serialize component state and event handlers into HTML; no replay of component tree on client

failureModes: Hydration mismatch errors: server HTML and client render produce different output; causes flicker or crash
Double-rendering: client re-executes all component logic that server already ran; wasted CPU
Layout shift: content moves when JS activates and changes dimensions of hydrated components

strategies.name: Invalidation Pattern
strategies.coreReframe: Define a strategy for keeping cached data fresh. Every cache is a bet that the data won't change before the next read; the invalidation pattern determines how and when that bet is settled.
structuralSignals: Stale data causing incorrect behavior or poor user experience
Cache consistency requirements: how fresh must the data be?
Write-through needs: writes must immediately reflect in cached reads
whatItUnlocks: Fresh reads without full recomputation: invalidate only what changed
Controlled staleness: TTL-based caching with known maximum age
Event-driven refresh: cache updated in response to write events, not polling
Read performance: cache absorbs read load while writes go to source of truth

classicExamples.examples: TTL-based: cache entries expire after a fixed duration; simple but allows staleness up to TTL
Event-driven invalidation: write event triggers cache delete or update; near-zero staleness
Write-through cache: every write updates both cache and backing store synchronously
Write-behind cache: writes go to cache immediately; asynchronously flushed to backing store

failureModes: Cache stampede: many clients simultaneously miss an expired key and hit the backing store; use lock or stale-while-revalidate
Missed invalidation: write path forgets to invalidate; stale data persists indefinitely
Thundering herd: popular key expires and all requests flood the origin; use jittered TTL or request coalescing

strategies.name: Zero Trust Approach
strategies.coreReframe: Verify every request regardless of network location. Never trust a request solely because it originates from the internal network; authenticate and authorize at every boundary.
structuralSignals: Internal service-to-service communication currently relies on network perimeter for security
Network perimeter is insufficient: cloud, VPN, remote workers blur the boundary
Lateral movement risk: a compromised internal service can access everything on the network
whatItUnlocks: Defense in depth: compromise of one service doesn't grant access to others
Microsegmentation: fine-grained access control per service, per endpoint, per action
Identity-based access: authentication tied to service identity, not network location
Audit trail: every authenticated request is logged with identity context

classicExamples.examples: mTLS: mutual TLS between services; both client and server present certificates
Service mesh (Istio): sidecar proxy handles mTLS, auth policy, and traffic control transparently
Identity-aware proxy: Google BeyondCorp — every request authenticated at the proxy regardless of network origin

failureModes: Certificate management overhead: mTLS requires CA infrastructure, cert rotation, and revocation handling
Latency from mutual auth: every request incurs TLS handshake cost; mitigate with connection pooling and session resumption
Complexity: zero trust adds configuration surface area; misconfigured policies can block legitimate traffic

strategies.name: Environment Promotion
strategies.coreReframe: Move immutable build artifacts through a sequence of environments (dev, staging, production) with increasing confidence gates. The same artifact is tested at each stage; only configuration changes between environments.
structuralSignals: Deployment pipeline with multiple environments before production
Environment parity concerns: what works in staging must work in production
Release approval gates: human or automated checks before promotion
whatItUnlocks: Repeatable deployments: same artifact, different config, predictable behavior
Audit trail: every promotion is recorded with who approved and what tests passed
Rollback capability: previous artifact is always available for re-deployment
Confidence escalation: each environment adds assurance before production

classicExamples.examples: GitOps (ArgoCD, Flux): desired state declared in Git; controller reconciles cluster state to match
Pipeline-driven (Jenkins, GitHub Actions): build once, deploy to dev, run tests, promote to staging, approve, promote to prod
Branch-per-environment: merge to 'staging' branch deploys to staging; merge to 'main' deploys to production

failureModes: Environment drift: staging and production diverge in config, data, or infrastructure; use infrastructure-as-code
Promotion without testing: skipping test gates under pressure; enforce gates in CI/CD pipeline
Manual hotfix bypasses: emergency changes skip the pipeline; create fast-track path that still runs critical tests

strategies.name: Sampling Approach
strategies.coreReframe: Decide which telemetry data to collect versus discard based on cost, volume, and debugging needs. Sampling controls the trade-off between observability completeness and resource consumption.
structuralSignals: High-volume telemetry exceeding storage or processing budget
Cost constraints: telemetry storage and ingestion costs growing faster than value
Debug vs production tracing needs: full traces needed for debugging but not for steady-state monitoring
whatItUnlocks: Cost control: collect a representative fraction instead of 100% of data
Manageable data volume: downstream systems (storage, indexing, querying) stay within capacity
Targeted debugging: increase sampling rate for specific services, errors, or slow requests
Adaptive capacity: sampling rate adjusts based on system load or error rate

classicExamples.examples: Head-based sampling: decide at trace start whether to sample (e.g., 10% of requests); simple, consistent per trace
Tail-based sampling: collect all spans, decide after trace completes whether to keep (e.g., keep errors and slow traces)
Adaptive sampling: increase sampling rate when error rate spikes or latency exceeds threshold
Always-on for errors: 100% sampling for error traces regardless of overall sampling rate

failureModes: Missing critical traces: the one request you need to debug was not sampled; use tail-based or error-aware sampling
Biased sampling: head-based sampling over-represents short traces; weight by duration or importance
Inconsistent sampling across services: parent sampled but child not, creating broken traces; propagate sampling decision in context

crossStrategyInteractionGuide.whatItProduces: CDQ divide & conquer: split queries in half, count cross contributions
Process deletions as additions in reverse; DSU with rollback
Binary search on the answer for a min-max problem after coordinate compression
Sort queries by right endpoint, sweep with BIT (offline range query pattern)
Lazy deletion in a heap is O(log n) amortized because stale entries are bounded by total inserts
Isolated modules with per-environment config injection enable independent testing and deployment
Factory-generated test data within sandboxed boundaries for deterministic integration tests
Expand-contract migrations that add denormalized read views before removing normalized originals
At-least-once delivery with idempotent consumers achieves exactly-once semantics under eventual consistency
Event-driven cache invalidation keeps denormalized views fresh after source data writes
Identity-verified promotions through environments with mTLS at every stage
Sampled real-user monitoring traces reveal which hydration strategy performs best in production
Coordinated API version and database schema evolution with backward-compatible transition periods
